ChatGPT said:

Good. Now we’re getting into actual engineering territory.

I’m not going to give you some textbook folder tree. I’ll give you a production-ready, scalable structure that won’t collapse when you add features later.

This is tailored for:

👉 Next.js (App Router) + Sanity + Vercel
👉 Company website (Zabnix)
👉 Scalable for future (multi-tenant / SaaS ready if needed)

🔥 FULL PROJECT STRUCTURE (Clean + Scalable)

zabnix/
│
├── app/                         # Next.js App Router
│   ├── layout.tsx
│   ├── page.tsx                # Homepage
│   │
│   ├── (routes)/               # Route grouping (clean URLs)
│   │   ├── team/
│   │   │   └── page.tsx
│   │   ├── services/
│   │   │   └── page.tsx
│   │   ├── projects/
│   │   │   └── page.tsx
│   │   ├── contact/
│   │   │   └── page.tsx
│   │
│   ├── admin/                  # Sanity Studio (embedded CMS)
│   │   └── [[...tool]]/page.tsx
│
├── components/                 # Reusable UI components
│   ├── common/
│   │   ├── Button.tsx
│   │   ├── Container.tsx
│   │   ├── Section.tsx
│   │
│   ├── layout/
│   │   ├── Navbar.tsx
│   │   ├── Footer.tsx
│   │
│   ├── sections/               # Page sections (modular)
│   │   ├── Hero.tsx
│   │   ├── Services.tsx
│   │   ├── TeamPreview.tsx
│   │   ├── ProjectsPreview.tsx
│   │   ├── CTA.tsx
│   │
│   ├── cards/                  # UI cards
│   │   ├── TeamCard.tsx
│   │   ├── ProjectCard.tsx
│   │   ├── ServiceCard.tsx
│
├── lib/                        # Core logic (VERY IMPORTANT)
│   ├── sanity/
│   │   ├── client.ts          # Sanity client config
│   │   ├── queries.ts         # GROQ queries
│   │   ├── image.ts           # image builder
│   │
│   ├── utils/
│   │   ├── helpers.ts
│   │   ├── constants.ts
│
├── sanity/                     # Sanity Studio setup
│   ├── schemaTypes/
│   │   ├── team.ts
│   │   ├── project.ts
│   │   ├── service.ts
│   │   ├── settings.ts
│   │
│   ├── sanity.config.ts
│   ├── structure.ts           # optional custom desk structure
│
├── hooks/                      # Custom React hooks
│   ├── useScroll.ts
│   ├── useLenis.ts
│
├── styles/
│   ├── globals.css
│
├── public/
│   ├── images/
│   ├── icons/
│
├── types/                      # TypeScript types
│   ├── team.ts
│   ├── project.ts
│   ├── service.ts
│
├── .env.local                  # Sanity keys
├── next.config.js
├── tailwind.config.js
├── tsconfig.json
└── package.json

🧠 WHY THIS STRUCTURE WORKS (no nonsense)

1. app/ → Routing Layer

  • Clean separation of pages

  • (routes) keeps URLs clean without nesting garbage

2. components/ → Modular UI

Split into:

  • layout → navbar/footer

  • sections → page blocks

  • cards → reusable UI

👉 This is what prevents spaghetti code later

3. lib/sanity/ → Brain of your CMS integration

This is critical.

client.ts

Connects to Sanity

queries.ts

All your GROQ queries live here

👉 NEVER mix queries inside components
That’s how projects become unmaintainable

4. sanity/ → CMS Definition

This defines:

  • Team schema

  • Projects

  • Services

👉 This is your “admin panel structure”

5. hooks/ → Behavior Layer

Perfect for:

  • Lenis scroll

  • Animations

  • Reusable logic

6. types/ → Type Safety

Don’t skip this.

👉 Prevents:

  • runtime bugs

  • API mismatch issues

⚙️ SANITY SCHEMA EXAMPLE (Team)

sanity/schemaTypes/team.ts
TypeScript
export default {
  name: "team",
  title: "Team",
  type: "document",
  fields: [
    { name: "name", type: "string" },
    { name: "role", type: "string" },
    { name: "image", type: "image" },
    { name: "bio", type: "text" },
    { name: "linkedin", type: "url" }
  ]
}

⚙️ SANITY CLIENT

lib/sanity/client.ts
TypeScript
import { createClient } from "next-sanity";

export const client = createClient({
  projectId: process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SANITY_PROJECT_ID,
  dataset: "production",
  apiVersion: "2024-01-01",
  useCdn: true,
});

⚙️ SAMPLE QUERY

lib/sanity/queries.ts
TypeScript
export const teamQuery = `*[_type == "team"]{
  _id,
  name,
  role,
  bio,
  image
}`;

⚙️ FETCH DATA IN PAGE

TypeScript
import { client } from "@/lib/sanity/client";
import { teamQuery } from "@/lib/sanity/queries";

export default async function TeamPage() {
  const team = await client.fetch(teamQuery);

  return (
    <div>
      {team.map((member) => (
        <div key={member._id}>{member.name}</div>
      ))}
    </div>
  );
}

🚀 PRO SETUP (DON’T SKIP)

1. Environment Variables

NEXT_PUBLIC_SANITY_PROJECT_ID=
NEXT_PUBLIC_SANITY_DATASET=production

2. Enable ISR (revalidation)

TypeScript
export const revalidate = 60;

👉 Keeps content fresh without rebuild

3. Lenis Hook (clean integration)

hooks/useLenis.ts

Keep it isolated → don’t pollute layout

⚠️ MISTAKES TO AVOID

Let me save you weeks of regret:

❌ Putting queries inside components
❌ Mixing UI + logic
❌ No type safety
❌ No folder discipline
❌ Overengineering CMS schema

💡 Final Reality Check

This structure is:

  • Simple enough to build fast

  • Strong enough to scale

  • Clean enough for team collaboration

👉 This is how real agency-level projects are structured

🚀 If you want next level

I can now:

👉 Build FULL starter repo (copy-paste ready)
👉 Include:

  • Navbar + Hero + Sections

  • Team page (dynamic)

  • Projects page

  • Sanity integration done

  • Lenis smooth scroll integrated

Just say:

👉 “give full starter code”

We’ll turn this into a working production site—not just folders.


You said:

what do we need from the peoples of the company not realted to the tech stacl

ChatGPT said:

This is the part most devs underestimate—and then the project gets delayed for weeks.

You don’t need tech from them.
You need content + clarity + authority.

Let’s break it down properly so you can go to your team and extract exactly what you need without confusion.

🔥 1. Company-Level Information (FOUNDATION)

Without this, your site will look generic.

You need:

  • Company name (exact legal + brand version)

  • Tagline (or at least raw idea)

  • What Zabnix actually does (in simple words, not jargon)

  • Vision / mission (even rough points)

👉 Your job:
Convert their messy explanation into sharp copy.

🧠 2. Services (THIS IS CRITICAL)

Don’t let them say:

“We do software”

Push them.

Extract:

  • Exact services list
    (e.g. Web apps, ERP, Mobile apps, Automation, AI, etc.)

  • Who it is for (clinics, startups, enterprises?)

  • Problems they solve

👉 Good websites are built around business problems, not services Wikipedia

👥 3. Team & Leadership Data (what you asked earlier)

For each person:

Mandatory:

  • Name

  • Role

  • Photo (clean, consistent)

Strongly recommended:

  • Short bio (2–3 lines max)

  • LinkedIn profile

  • Expertise / skills

For Leadership (important difference):

  • Authority statement
    (e.g. “10+ years in healthcare operations”)

👉 This builds trust instantly.

🧩 4. Projects / Work / Portfolio

This is where most companies fail.

Don’t ask:

“Give project details”

Ask like this:

For each project:

  • What problem was solved?

  • What solution was built?

  • What tech used (optional)

  • Result / impact (VERY important)

👉 Even internal tools count.

🏢 5. Parent Company Connection (your advantage)

You’re not random.

You’re connected to:
👉 Sahakar Medicals & Surgicals

So you need:

  • How Zabnix supports them

  • Any real-world systems you built

  • Scale of operations (even rough numbers)

👉 This = credibility layer

📞 6. Contact & Business Info

Simple but essential:

  • Phone numbers

  • Email

  • Office address

  • WhatsApp (if used)

  • Working hours

🎯 7. Branding Assets (DON’T SKIP)

You need:

  • Logo (SVG preferred)

  • Brand colors (if not, you define)

  • Any existing brochures / docs

  • Photos (office, work, team)

🧠 8. Content Ownership (VERY IMPORTANT)

Decide this early:

👉 Who will update content?

Because a website needs:

  • content owners

  • not just developers

Organizations usually assign stakeholders responsible for maintaining and updating website content Wikipedia

⚠️ Brutal Reality (you need to handle this)

People will:

  • Delay giving data

  • Give incomplete info

  • Send low-quality images

  • Write garbage descriptions

👉 If you wait for perfect input, project dies.

💡 Smart Move (what you should do)

Instead of asking everything:

👉 Create a Google Form / Sheet with fields:

  • Name

  • Role

  • Bio

  • Photo upload

  • LinkedIn

👉 Force structure.

🚀 What YOU are responsible for

Let’s be clear:

They provide:
👉 Raw info

YOU convert it into:
👉 Clean, structured, high-impact content

🧩 Final Thought

Tech is easy.
Content is the real bottleneck.

Most websites don’t fail because of code.
They fail because:
👉 nobody knew what to say.

🚀 If you want next step

I can:

👉 Create a ready-to-use content collection template (Google Form + Sheet format)
👉 Tailored for Zabnix
👉 So you just send link → collect everything cleanly

Just say:
👉 “give content collection template”

That’ll save you days of back-and-forth headache.


You said:

i wna to give this to a responsible perso so give something like it

ChatGPT said:

Perfect—this is exactly how you handle it like a professional.
You don’t “ask randomly,” you assign responsibility with structure.

I’ll give you something you can copy-paste and hand over to a responsible person (HR / Admin / Manager). This is not fluffy—it’s operational.

📄 CONTENT COLLECTION DOCUMENT (Give this as-is)

ZABNIX WEBSITE – CONTENT COLLECTION SHEET

This document is to collect all required information for building the Zabnix company website. Please fill all fields carefully and completely. Incomplete or unclear data will delay the project.

1. COMPANY INFORMATION

  • Full Company Name:

  • Brand Name (if different):

  • Tagline (if any):

  • Short Description (2–3 lines about what Zabnix does):

  • Vision (optional):

  • Mission (optional):

2. SERVICES

List all services provided by Zabnix.

For each service:

  • Service Name:

  • Description (simple, non-technical):

  • Target Audience (who is this for?):

  • Problem it solves:

(Add multiple entries as needed)

3. TEAM & MANAGEMENT DETAILS

For each person:

  • Full Name:

  • Role / Designation:

  • Department (optional):

  • Short Bio (2–3 lines only):

  • Key Skills / Expertise (optional):

  • LinkedIn Profile (if available):

  • Photo (upload separately – good quality, formal preferred):

⚠️ Note:

  • Leadership (CEO, Directors, Heads) must have stronger bios

  • Avoid long paragraphs

4. PROJECTS / WORKS / PORTFOLIO

For each project:

  • Project Name:

  • Type (Web App / Mobile App / ERP / etc.):

  • Problem Faced:

  • Solution Provided:

  • Outcome / Result (very important):

  • Technologies Used (optional):

  • Screenshots / Images (if available):

5. PARENT COMPANY CONNECTION

  • How is Zabnix related to Sahakar Medicals & Surgicals?

  • Any systems/software built for them:

  • Scale of operations (approx numbers if available):

6. CONTACT INFORMATION

  • Official Email:

  • Phone Number(s):

  • WhatsApp Number (if used):

  • Office Address:

  • Working Hours:

7. BRANDING MATERIALS

  • Logo (SVG / PNG):

  • Brand Colors (if known):

  • Any brochures / company documents:

  • Office / work photos (optional):

8. CONTENT APPROVAL RESPONSIBILITY

  • Name of person responsible for final approval:

  • Role:

  • Contact:

IMPORTANT INSTRUCTIONS

  • Do not leave fields blank unless optional

  • Avoid technical jargon—keep explanations simple

  • Ensure all images are clear and professional

  • Submit all data in one go to avoid delays

Deadline for submission: __________

💡 How to Use This (practical advice)

Don’t just send and hope.

👉 Do this:

  • Send it via Google Docs / Google Form

  • Assign ONE responsible person (not multiple people)

  • Give a deadline

  • Follow up once—don’t chase forever

⚠️ Reality Check

If you don’t structure this:

  • You’ll get half data

  • WhatsApp photos

  • Random descriptions

  • Endless back-and-forth

👉 Project delay guaranteed.

🚀 Pro Move (if you want next level)

I can convert this into:

👉 Google Form (auto-organized)
👉 Excel sheet format
👉 Directly mapped to your Sanity CMS schema

So you can literally:
👉 Import → Done

Just say:
👉 “convert this to form + sanity mapping”

That’s how you move from “developer” to “system builder.”

Now let’s define the REAL identity of the website.

🔥 PHASE 1 — BRAND FOUNDATION

Answer these one by one properly.

1. What exactly is Zabnix?

Not legally.
Not technically.

In HUMAN language.

Example:

“We build scalable software solutions for businesses.”

OR

“We create modern web, mobile, and automation systems.”

What’s YOUR version?

2. What are the MAIN SERVICES?

List everything raw. Don’t organize yet.

Example:

  • Web development

  • ERP

  • Mobile apps

  • AI automation

  • UI/UX

  • Hosting

  • Cloud

  • API integrations

  • etc

Dump everything.

3. Who is your TARGET CLIENT?

VERY important.

Which of these?

  • Startups

  • Hospitals

  • Pharmacies

  • Enterprises

  • Local businesses

  • Educational institutions

  • Internal Sahakar ecosystem

  • Everyone (bad answer usually)

4. What vibe should the site give?

Choose one primary direction:

A.

Minimal + premium
(Stripe / Linear vibe)

B.

Bold + animated
(Awwwards agency vibe)

C.

Corporate + trust-heavy
(TCS / Infosys vibe)

D.

Futuristic tech
(3D + glow + motion heavy)

E.

Hybrid of above

5. Dark theme or Light theme?

Or:

  • Dark primary

  • Light primary

  • Auto switch

6. Do you want:

Smooth cinematic experience?

(Lenis + transitions + motion)

OR

Fast minimal professional?

(Light animation only)

7. Pages you definitely need

Choose properly.

Example:

  • Home

  • About

  • Services

  • Projects

  • Team

  • Careers

  • Contact

  • Blog

  • Case studies

  • Products

  • Solutions

  • Industries

8. What should the homepage focus MOST on?

Choose priority:

  • Getting leads

  • Showing portfolio

  • Building credibility

  • Explaining services

  • Recruitment

  • Investor/company image

9. Do you already have:

  • Logo?

  • Brand colors?

  • Fonts?

  • Brand guideline?

If yes, share details.

10. Do you want CMS control for:

(check all)

  • Team

  • Projects

  • Services

  • Homepage sections

  • Testimonials

  • Careers

  • Blog

  • SEO

Answer these first.

Then I’ll move to:
✅ Site architecture
✅ UI direction
✅ Animation strategy
✅ Sanity schema planning
✅ Folder structure refinement
✅ Component system
✅ Full implementation roadmap

We’re doing this properly.

🔥 WHAT ZABNIX SHOULD FEEL LIKE

Based on your answers:

Brand Personality

  • Futuristic

  • Intelligent

  • Bold

  • Cinematic

  • Premium

  • Technically strong

  • Enterprise capable

NOT:

  • playful startup

  • cartoon SaaS

  • generic dev agency

🎯 FINAL CREATIVE DIRECTION

Core Blend

Visual Style

👉 Linear + Vercel + Awwwards-inspired motion

Brand Tone

👉 Enterprise-grade innovation

Experience

👉 Smooth cinematic scrolling
(Lenis + layered animations + transitions)

🔥 SITE EXPERIENCE PLAN

Homepage Experience

The homepage should feel like:

entering a digital ecosystem

Not:

reading a brochure

🧠 HOMEPAGE STRUCTURE (MASTER PLAN)

1. Hero Section

FULLSCREEN cinematic intro.

Content:

  • Bold statement

  • Animated grid / glow / particles

  • Subtle 3D depth

  • Motion-reactive background

Example direction:

“Building Scalable Digital Systems for Modern Businesses”

Buttons:

  • Explore Services

  • Start a Project

2. Trusted Ecosystem Section

This is where Sahakar backing enters.

NOT pharmacy-focused.

Instead:

“Built with real-world operational experience.”

This subtly increases trust.

3. Services Section

Animated cards.

Your services become:

  • Web Systems

  • Enterprise ERP

  • Mobile Applications

  • AI Automation

  • Cloud Infrastructure

  • API Ecosystems

NOT boring icon boxes.

4. Featured Projects

Case-study style.

Cinematic scrolling.
Large previews.
Hover interactions.

5. Industries

This is important because you target many sectors.

Industries:

  • Healthcare

  • Education

  • Enterprise

  • Retail

  • Startups

  • Internal Systems

6. Products

This increases perceived company maturity massively.

Even internal tools can appear here.

7. Team / Leadership

Premium layout.
Motion-heavy transitions.
Not LinkedIn-style boring grids.

8. Careers

Culture + innovation-focused.

9. Contact CTA

Large cinematic CTA near footer.

🎨 DESIGN LANGUAGE

UI Style

  • Dark futuristic base

  • Glow accents

  • Glassmorphism (minimal)

  • Noise textures

  • Gradient lighting

  • Motion layers

⚠️ IMPORTANT

You said:

“3D + glow + motion heavy”

This can go VERY wrong.

Most devs make:
❌ laggy
❌ unreadable
❌ overanimated
❌ GPU-burning messes

We need:
✅ controlled motion
✅ strategic animation
✅ performance-first cinematic feel

🧠 TECHNOLOGY DECISIONS (LOCKED)

Stack

  • Next.js App Router

  • TypeScript

  • Tailwind

  • Framer Motion

  • Lenis

  • GSAP (selectively)

  • Sanity CMS

  • Vercel

🔥 ANIMATION STRATEGY

Use Lenis for:

  • smooth scroll

  • cinematic flow

Use Framer Motion for:

  • UI transitions

  • entrance animations

Use GSAP ONLY for:

  • hero section

  • advanced scroll scenes

NOT everywhere.

🎯 NOW WE DEFINE THE ACTUAL BRAND

Next phase.

Answer these carefully.

🔥 PHASE 2 — BRAND IDENTITY

1. Exact Logo Type

Which do you have?

  • Icon logo

  • Wordmark

  • Combination mark

2. Brand Colors

Give:

  • Primary color

  • Secondary color

  • Accent/glow color

Hex if possible.

3. Fonts

Which fonts are in the guideline?

4. What emotions should users feel?

Choose max 3:

  • Trust

  • Innovation

  • Speed

  • Intelligence

  • Premium quality

  • Futuristic

  • Stability

  • Creativity

  • Power

  • Reliability

5. Do you want:

Minimal premium motion

OR

Aggressive cinematic animation

Be honest.

6. Homepage Hero Preference

Which direction?

A.

Huge typography + subtle motion

B.

3D futuristic scene

C.

Particle / glow ecosystem

D.

Video-style cinematic panels

E.

Hybrid

7. Any websites you personally LOVE?

Even unrelated ones.

Send:

  • company names

  • links

  • screenshots

This matters A LOT.

8. Should the site feel more:

Corporate

OR

Experimental

9. Do you want blog articles to help SEO?

(Highly recommended)

10. Do you want career applications directly through website?

(upload resume etc)

Once you answer this:
I’ll move into:
✅ exact sitemap
✅ exact section hierarchy
✅ animation map

Then we start coding.


You said:

 this is the logo and font and name

ChatGPT said:

Now this helps a LOT.

The logo already tells me something important:

👉 Zabnix is leaning toward a clean tech identity, not a chaotic cyberpunk identity.

That’s good. Because the biggest mistake people make with “futuristic” is turning the website into a gaming intro instead of a professional software company.

🔥 BRAND ANALYSIS (Based on Logo)

What the logo communicates

✅ Strong points

  • Bold typography

  • Minimal

  • Memorable

  • Modern

  • Enterprise-capable

  • Flexible branding system

What the logo DOESN’T support

❌ Overly neon cyberpunk
❌ Crazy RGB everywhere
❌ Heavy holographic UI
❌ Too much glassmorphism

That would clash badly.

🎯 REAL DESIGN DIRECTION (Now Clear)

Your actual visual identity should become:

“Minimal Futuristic Enterprise”

Think:

  • Linear

  • Vercel

  • Stripe

  • Some Awwwards motion

BUT NOT:

  • sci-fi gamer website

🧠 VERY IMPORTANT REALIZATION

Your logo is:

BLACK + CLEAN + TYPOGRAPHIC

That means:
👉 typography becomes your biggest design weapon.

Not:

  • giant 3D robots

  • useless particles

  • random animations

🔥 FINAL VISUAL DIRECTION

Core Style

Minimal dark futuristic

With:

  • cinematic motion

  • premium transitions

  • subtle glow systems

  • strong typography

  • layered depth

🎨 COLOR STRATEGY

Since your logo is monochrome:

Recommended palette

Primary

#000000

Background

#0A0A0A

Surface

#111111

Accent Glow

Choose ONE:

  • Electric Blue

  • Violet

  • Cyan

NOT all three.

⚠️ IMPORTANT

Do NOT make:

  • full gradient backgrounds

  • rainbow glows

  • excessive blur

Your logo demands:
👉 controlled elegance.

🧠 FONT ANALYSIS

The typography style already feels:

  • geometric

  • modern

  • premium

You should pair it with:

  • Inter

  • Satoshi

  • General Sans

  • Geist

NOT futuristic gimmick fonts.

🔥 NOW YOUR WEBSITE STYLE BECOMES:

Homepage Feel

Hero:

Massive typography.
Slow cinematic movement.
Minimal but deep.

Motion:

  • smooth

  • layered

  • responsive

  • premium

NOT:

  • hyperactive

🎯 THIS IS THE PERFECT COMBINATION FOR YOU

Visual DNA

40% Linear
25% Vercel
20% Apple
15% Awwwards motion

That’s your sweet spot.

This helps:

⚠️ IMPORTANT DESIGN WARNING

You wanted:

futuristic + cinematic

Here’s the reality:

90% of “futuristic” sites:

  • lag

  • feel fake

  • destroy readability

  • ruin mobile UX

We are NOT doing that.

🧠 OUR MOTION RULE

Animation should:
✅ support storytelling
✅ create depth
✅ guide attention

NOT:
❌ scream for attention

- Admin dashboards

ERP
- Inventory
- HR
- Billing

Break ALL services like this.

  • internal tools

  • WIP apps

  • dashboards

  • ERP systems

  • AI tools

Even unfinished.

And what types?

  • ERP

  • Website

  • Mobile app

  • Internal software

  • AI automation

Example:

  • Frontend developer

  • Backend developer

  • remote?

  • hybrid?

  • onsite?

More:

  • technical developer articles
    OR

  • Book a meeting

  • WhatsApp

  • Start a project

  • Contact sales

Should it focus more on:

  • company story

  • technology vision

  • innovation

  • ecosystem backing

  • leadership

9. Do you want:

Animated section transitions between pages?

Example:

  • smooth route transitions

  • cinematic wipes

YES or NO?

Should mobile:

  • keep cinematic effects
    OR

  • reduce effects for speed?

(Important decision.)

1.PRD 2. TRD 5. Backend schema 3. Ul/UX Design 6. Implementation plan 4. Appflow give me these

ChatGPT said:

Perfect. Now we’re entering the actual product planning stage.

And these 6 things are exactly the correct order.
Most people jump into UI first and create a mess later.

The correct order is:

1. PRD
2. TRD
3. UI/UX Design
4. App Flow
5. Backend Schema
6. Implementation Plan

That’s how real systems are built.

Now let’s define what each one means specifically for Zabnix.

ChatGPT said:

ZABNIX WEBSITE — PRODUCT REQUIREMENTS DOCUMENT (PRD)

1. Project Overview

Project Name

ZABNIX Website Platform

Company

ZABNIX — Sahakar IT Solution

Project Type

Corporate Software Company Website with CMS Management

Website Goal

To establish ZABNIX as a futuristic, scalable, enterprise-grade software engineering company capable of delivering modern digital systems, enterprise platforms, AI automation, and cloud-based solutions.

The website should:

  • Generate qualified leads

  • Build trust and authority

  • Showcase products and projects

  • Present team and leadership

  • Support recruitment

  • Publish technical and industry content

  • Position ZABNIX as a serious technology company

2. Business Objectives

Primary Objectives

  • Generate inbound business leads

  • Showcase technical capability

  • Build digital brand identity

  • Present portfolio and products

  • Enable recruitment workflows

Secondary Objectives

  • Improve online visibility and SEO

  • Publish technical blogs and case studies

  • Establish long-term authority in software engineering

  • Support ecosystem credibility through Sahakar connection

3. Target Audience

Primary Audience

  • Startups

  • SMEs

  • Enterprises

  • Healthcare organizations

  • Educational institutions

  • Internal operational ecosystems

Secondary Audience

  • Job seekers

  • Technical talent

  • Investors/partners

  • Industry collaborators

4. Brand Positioning

Core Positioning

“Building scalable digital systems for modern businesses.”

Brand Personality

  • Futuristic

  • Professional

  • Intelligent

  • Bold

  • Premium

  • Technically capable

  • Enterprise-oriented

Brand Tone

  • Clear

  • Technical but understandable

  • Confident

  • Modern

  • Minimalistic

5. Website Experience Goals

The website should feel:

  • Cinematic

  • Smooth

  • Futuristic

  • Interactive

  • Modern

  • Fast

  • Premium

Motion and animation should support storytelling and user engagement without affecting usability or performance.

6. Core Features

6.1 Homepage

Purpose

Serve as the primary landing and conversion page.

Includes

  • Cinematic hero section

  • Service highlights

  • Industry sectors

  • Products showcase

  • Featured projects

  • Team preview

  • Testimonials

  • Contact CTA

6.2 About Page

Purpose

Present company identity and ecosystem credibility.

Includes

  • Company overview

  • Vision/Mission (future)

  • Leadership

  • Sahakar ecosystem relationship

  • Company culture

6.3 Services Page

Purpose

Show detailed service offerings.

Includes

  • Web development

  • ERP systems

  • Mobile applications

  • AI automation

  • Cloud solutions

  • API integrations

  • UI/UX systems

Each service should include:

  • Overview

  • Use cases

  • Benefits

  • Related industries

6.4 Solutions Page

Purpose

Present problem-solving capabilities.

Includes

  • Business automation

  • Enterprise systems

  • Digital transformation

  • Workflow optimization

6.5 Industries Page

Purpose

Show industry specialization.

Industries

  • Healthcare

  • Education

  • Retail

  • Enterprise

  • Startups

  • Operations

6.6 Products Page

Purpose

Show proprietary/internal products and WIP systems.

Includes

  • Internal tools

  • ERP systems

  • Dashboards

  • SaaS/WIP products

6.7 Projects Page

Purpose

Portfolio and capability showcase.

Includes

  • Case-study style project display

  • Screenshots

  • Technology stack

  • Results/outcomes

6.8 Case Studies

Purpose

Detailed storytelling around implementations.

Includes

  • Problem

  • Process

  • Solution

  • Result

6.9 Team Page

Purpose

Present leadership and technical team.

Structure

  • Leadership section

  • Development team

  • Design team

  • Operations

6.10 Careers Page

Purpose

Recruitment and talent acquisition.

Features

  • CMS-managed job postings

  • Job detail pages

  • Resume upload

  • Application forms

6.11 Blog / Insights

Purpose

SEO, authority, technical communication.

Categories

  • Technical blogs

  • AI insights

  • Company updates

  • Engineering articles

  • Case studies

6.12 Contact Page

Purpose

Lead conversion and communication.

Features

  • Contact form

  • WhatsApp integration

  • Calendly booking

  • Direct call

  • Email communication

7. CMS Requirements

The system must allow non-technical content management through Sanity CMS.

CMS Modules

Mandatory

  • Homepage content

  • Services

  • Projects

  • Products

  • Team

  • Leadership

  • Careers

  • Blog

  • SEO

  • Testimonials

  • Contact settings

8. User Flows

Lead Generation Flow

Landing
→ Explore Services
→ View Projects
→ Trust Building
→ Contact/Book Meeting

Recruitment Flow

Careers
→ Job Listing
→ Job Details
→ Apply
→ Resume Submission

Content Flow

Blog
→ Article
→ Related Content
→ Contact CTA

9. UI/UX Requirements

Design Style

Minimal Futuristic Enterprise

Visual Identity

  • Dark theme

  • Electric blue accent glow

  • Large typography

  • Smooth motion

  • Layered visual depth

Motion System

Includes

  • Lenis smooth scrolling

  • Framer Motion transitions

  • GSAP cinematic hero animations

Excludes

  • Excessive motion

  • Distracting effects

  • Performance-heavy visuals

10. Technical Requirements

Frontend

  • Next.js

  • TypeScript

  • Tailwind CSS

Animation

  • Framer Motion

  • Lenis

  • GSAP

CMS

  • Sanity

Hosting

  • Vercel

11. SEO Requirements

Must Include

  • Metadata management

  • OpenGraph support

  • Sitemap generation

  • Structured schema

  • Dynamic SEO fields in CMS

12. Performance Requirements

Target

  • Lighthouse score above 90

  • Mobile optimized

  • Lazy loading

  • Optimized images

  • Smooth motion without lag

13. Accessibility Requirements

The website should:

  • Support keyboard navigation

  • Maintain readable contrast

  • Be mobile responsive

  • Use semantic HTML

14. Security Requirements

Includes

  • Spam protection for forms

  • Secure CMS access

  • File validation for resumes/uploads

15. Future Scalability

The architecture should support future:

  • SaaS systems

  • Client portals

  • Admin systems

  • Authentication layers

  • Multi-language support

16. Success Metrics

KPIs

  • Lead submissions

  • Meeting bookings

  • Career applications

  • Blog traffic

  • SEO growth

  • Portfolio engagement

  • Contact conversions

17. Final Product Vision

The final website should:

  • Feel premium and futuristic

  • Present ZABNIX as a serious engineering company

  • Create strong visual identity

  • Generate business trust

  • Scale with future company growth


You said:

trd

ChatGPT said:

ZABNIX WEBSITE — TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS DOCUMENT (TRD)

1. Technical Overview

Project Name

ZABNIX Website Platform

Project Type

Headless CMS-driven corporate software company website

Architecture Type

Decoupled frontend + cloud CMS architecture

2. System Architecture

High-Level Architecture

Client Browser
      ↓
Next.js Frontend (Vercel)
      ↓
Sanity CMS API
      ↓
Sanity Content Lake

Architecture Goals

The system must:

  • Scale efficiently

  • Support dynamic content

  • Maintain high performance

  • Allow non-technical content editing

  • Support future extensibility

3. Technology Stack

Frontend Framework

Next.js 15

Purpose:

  • App Router architecture

  • SSR/ISR support

  • SEO optimization

  • Performance optimization

Language

TypeScript

Purpose:

  • Type safety

  • Better scalability

  • Reduced runtime errors

Styling

Tailwind CSS

Purpose:

  • Utility-first styling

  • Rapid UI development

  • Design consistency

Animation Stack

Framer Motion

Used for:

  • Component animations

  • Route transitions

  • Reveal animations

Lenis

Used for:

  • Smooth scrolling

  • Cinematic motion flow

GSAP

Used selectively for:

  • Hero animations

  • Scroll timelines

  • Advanced motion sequences

GSAP usage must remain limited to avoid performance issues.

4. CMS Architecture

CMS Platform

Sanity

Purpose:

  • Dynamic content management

  • Real-time editing

  • Structured content

  • Cloud-managed backend

Sanity supports real-time APIs and structured content workflows.

5. Hosting Infrastructure

Frontend Hosting

Vercel

Purpose:

  • Global CDN delivery

  • Automatic deployments

  • Next.js optimization

  • Edge performance

Vercel provides optimized deployment support for Next.js applications.

CMS Hosting

Sanity Cloud Infrastructure

Purpose:

  • Content storage

  • API delivery

  • Asset management

6. Rendering Strategy

Static Site Generation (SSG)

Used for:

  • Homepage

  • Services

  • About pages

Incremental Static Regeneration (ISR)

Used for:

  • Blogs

  • Projects

  • Careers

  • Dynamic CMS content

Dynamic Rendering

Used only when necessary.

Avoid excessive server rendering to maintain performance.

7. Frontend Architecture

App Router Structure

app/
├── layout.tsx
├── page.tsx
├── about/
├── services/
├── projects/
├── blog/
├── careers/
├── contact/
├── products/
├── industries/
├── solutions/
├── team/

8. Component Architecture

Component Categories

Layout Components

Navbar
Footer
Container
Section
PageWrapper

Section Components

Hero
ServicesSection
ProjectsSection
TeamSection
CTASection

UI Components

Button
Card
Input
Modal
Badge
Tabs
GlowEffects

9. Design System

Theme Modes

  • Dark mode

  • Light mode

  • System auto-switch

Design Tokens

Colors

Primary: #000000
Background: #0A0A0A
Surface: #111111
Accent: Electric Blue
Text: #FFFFFF
Muted: #A1A1AA

Typography

Recommended:

  • Geist

  • Inter

  • Satoshi

Spacing System

Use:

  • 4px base spacing scale

10. Animation Architecture

Motion Principles

Animations must:

  • Enhance storytelling

  • Guide attention

  • Maintain readability

  • Preserve performance

Scroll System

Lenis Integration

Features:

  • Smooth scroll interpolation

  • Cinematic flow

  • Controlled easing

Motion Categories

Micro Interactions

  • Hover effects

  • Button animations

  • Card motion

Section Transitions

  • Fade

  • Slide

  • Scale reveal

Cinematic Motion

  • Hero sequences

  • Scroll-driven effects

  • Layer transitions

11. CMS Schema Architecture

Collections

settings
homepage
services
solutions
industries
products
projects
caseStudies
team
leadership
careers
blog
seo
testimonials
contact

12. Schema Definitions

Team Schema

name
role
bio
image
linkedin
department
featured
order

Project Schema

title
slug
description
coverImage
gallery
technologies
industry
status
featured
links

Blog Schema

title
slug
excerpt
content
author
category
coverImage
seo
publishedAt

Careers Schema

title
slug
department
type
location
description
requirements
applicationEmail
isOpen

13. API Layer

Data Source

Sanity Content API

Query Language

GROQ

Used for:

  • Structured querying

  • Dynamic filtering

  • Content fetching

Sanity uses GROQ for structured content queries.

14. SEO Architecture

Features

  • Dynamic metadata

  • OpenGraph tags

  • Twitter cards

  • Sitemap generation

  • Robots.txt

  • Structured data

Schema Markup

Use:

  • Organization schema

  • Article schema

  • JobPosting schema

  • Breadcrumb schema

15. Performance Requirements

Lighthouse Targets

MetricTarget
Performance90+
Accessibility90+
Best Practices90+
SEO95+

Optimization Strategies

Images

  • Next Image optimization

  • WebP/AVIF formats

  • Lazy loading

Fonts

  • Local font hosting

  • Font optimization

Motion

  • GPU-friendly transforms

  • Avoid layout thrashing

  • Reduce excessive blur

16. Mobile Optimization

Mobile Strategy

Mobile-first responsive architecture.

Mobile Motion Rules

Reduce:

  • heavy particles

  • excessive blur

  • large GSAP timelines

Maintain:

  • smooth UX

  • readability

  • speed

17. Security Requirements

Form Protection

  • Honeypot fields

  • Validation

  • Spam prevention

CMS Security

  • Role-based access

  • Protected studio access

  • Environment variable isolation

18. Accessibility Requirements

Requirements

  • Semantic HTML

  • Keyboard navigation

  • Proper contrast ratios

  • Screen-reader support

Accessibility compliance improves usability and SEO.

19. File Structure

Core Structure

app/
components/
lib/
hooks/
styles/
types/
sanity/
public/

20. Deployment Pipeline

Version Control

GitHub

CI/CD

Vercel automatic deployment

Workflow:

Git Push
↓
Automatic Build
↓
Deployment
↓
Production Release

21. Environment Variables

Required

NEXT_PUBLIC_SANITY_PROJECT_ID
NEXT_PUBLIC_SANITY_DATASET
SANITY_API_TOKEN
NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL

22. Logging & Monitoring

Monitoring

Recommended:

  • Vercel Analytics

  • Google Analytics

  • Sentry

23. Future Scalability

Architecture must support future:

  • authentication systems

  • client dashboards

  • SaaS modules

  • multilingual support

  • API expansion

  • tenant systems

24. Technical Constraints

Avoid

  • Heavy Three.js scenes

  • Excessive GSAP timelines

  • Large unoptimized assets

  • Over-rendering

  • Deep component nesting

25. Final Technical Vision

The platform should:

  • feel futuristic

  • maintain enterprise stability

  • scale cleanly

  • remain highly performant

  • support cinematic interaction without sacrificing usability

Sanity provides structured content APIs and real-time content capabilities suitable for headless CMS workflows.

Next.js and Vercel together provide optimized frontend deployment and rendering workflows for modern React applications.


You said:

Ul/UX Design

ChatGPT said:

ZABNIX WEBSITE — UI/UX DESIGN DOCUMENT

1. UI/UX Overview

Design Objective

Create a futuristic, cinematic, enterprise-grade digital experience that positions ZABNIX as a premium software engineering company.

The interface must:

  • feel modern and intelligent

  • create visual authority

  • communicate technical capability

  • remain usable and performant

  • balance cinematic motion with enterprise clarity

2. Design Philosophy

Core Philosophy

“Minimal Futuristic Enterprise”

The design should feel:

  • premium

  • immersive

  • technically advanced

  • clean

  • controlled

NOT:

  • noisy

  • cyberpunk

  • gaming-oriented

  • overanimated

3. Visual Identity System

Brand Mood

Emotional Goals

Users should feel:

  • trust

  • innovation

  • intelligence

  • scalability

  • confidence

4. Color System

Primary Palette

UsageColor
Primary Background#0A0A0A
Surface#111111
Elevated Surface#171717
Primary Text#FFFFFF
Secondary Text#A1A1AA
Borderrgba(255,255,255,0.08)
Accent GlowElectric Blue
Accent Secondary#3B82F6

Glow System

Glow usage must remain:

  • subtle

  • strategic

  • layered

Use glow only for:

  • CTA buttons

  • active states

  • hover interactions

  • section highlights

  • motion accents

Avoid:

  • full-screen glow flooding

  • excessive blur

  • RGB effects

5. Typography System

Typography Direction

Large, bold, cinematic typography.

Typography becomes the primary visual storytelling element.

Font Stack

Primary Font

  • Geist

  • Inter

  • Satoshi

Typography Rules

Headlines

  • large scale

  • tight hierarchy

  • strong contrast

Body Text

  • clean

  • readable

  • high spacing consistency

Typography Scale

ElementSize
Hero Display72–120px
Section Heading40–64px
Card Title20–28px
Body Text16–18px
Small Labels12–14px

6. Layout System

Layout Philosophy

Use:

  • spacious layouts

  • cinematic breathing room

  • layered depth

Avoid:

  • crowded interfaces

  • dense grids

  • excessive information stacking

Grid System

Desktop

  • 12-column grid

Tablet

  • 8-column grid

Mobile

  • 4-column grid

Section Spacing

DeviceSection Gap
Desktop120–180px
Tablet80–120px
Mobile64–96px

7. Motion Design System

Motion Philosophy

Motion should:

  • guide attention

  • create immersion

  • enhance storytelling

  • support transitions

Motion should NEVER:

  • distract

  • reduce readability

  • impact usability

8. Scroll Experience

Lenis Smooth Scroll

Purpose

Create cinematic movement flow.

Scroll Feel

  • smooth

  • controlled

  • elegant

  • slightly weighted

Avoid:

  • overfloaty behavior

  • exaggerated easing

9. Animation Categories

Micro Interactions

Includes

  • hover glow

  • card elevation

  • button transitions

  • icon movement

Duration:

150ms – 300ms

Section Reveals

Includes

  • fade-up

  • stagger reveal

  • opacity transitions

  • subtle blur entrance

Duration:

400ms – 800ms

Cinematic Motion

Used for

  • hero section

  • loader

  • page transitions

  • storytelling scenes

Duration:

800ms – 2000ms

10. Hero Section UX

Hero Goals

Immediately communicate:

  • capability

  • innovation

  • scale

  • premium identity

Hero Structure

Layer 1

Animated grid / cinematic background

Layer 2

Glow system + particles

Layer 3

Main typography

Layer 4

CTA buttons

Layer 5

Subtle ecosystem visualization

11. Navigation UX

Navbar Behavior

Desktop

  • floating transparent navbar

  • blur backdrop

  • transforms on scroll

Mobile

  • fullscreen menu overlay

  • cinematic transition

  • large navigation typography

Navigation Structure

Home
About
Services
Projects
Products
Careers
Blog
Contact

12. Page Transition UX

Transition Goals

Pages should feel connected like a system.

Transition Style

  • smooth fade

  • directional motion

  • cinematic wipe

  • subtle scaling

Avoid:

  • flashy route animations

  • long blocking transitions

13. Homepage UX Structure

Flow

Hero
↓
Ecosystem
↓
Services
↓
Industries
↓
Products
↓
Projects
↓
Team
↓
Blog
↓
Contact CTA

14. Card Design System

Card Style

Minimal futuristic panels.

Card Features

  • soft glow borders

  • layered surfaces

  • hover elevation

  • animated highlights

Card Types

Service Cards

Horizontal cinematic cards.

Project Cards

Large immersive showcase cards.

Team Cards

Premium portrait-focused layout.

15. Team UX

Leadership

  • larger cards

  • dominant hierarchy

  • stronger typography

Team Members

  • smaller modular grid

  • simplified interaction

Interaction

Optional:

  • modal profile expansion

  • hover depth effects

16. Projects UX

Project Display Style

Case-study cinematic presentation.

Includes

  • fullscreen imagery

  • smooth reveal

  • technology stack

  • result metrics

17. Careers UX

Goals

Recruit skilled technical talent.

UX Flow

Job Listings
↓
Job Details
↓
Apply
↓
Resume Upload

Career Page Feel

  • innovative

  • engineering-focused

  • growth-oriented

18. Blog UX

Reading Experience

Must prioritize:

  • readability

  • typography

  • spacing

Blog Features

  • related posts

  • category navigation

  • progress indicator

  • share actions

19. Contact UX

Contact Priorities

Primary CTA:

Start a Project

Contact Methods

  • Email

  • WhatsApp

  • Calendly

  • Direct Call

Contact Experience

Should feel:

  • easy

  • premium

  • frictionless

20. Loading Experience

Loader Style

Minimal cinematic loader.

Features

  • logo animation

  • glow pulse

  • subtle transition

Maximum duration:

2 seconds

21. Responsive UX Strategy

Mobile Philosophy

Mobile should:

  • preserve cinematic identity

  • reduce heavy effects

  • prioritize speed

Mobile Adjustments

Reduce:

  • particles

  • blur

  • heavy animations

  • large GSAP timelines

Maintain:

  • smooth transitions

  • typography hierarchy

  • premium feel

22. Accessibility UX

Requirements

  • readable contrast

  • keyboard navigation

  • focus states

  • semantic structure

23. Performance UX Rules

Avoid

  • animation overload

  • large video backgrounds

  • excessive 3D scenes

  • GPU-heavy rendering

Prioritize

  • smoothness

  • responsiveness

  • clarity

  • mobile stability

24. UX Success Metrics

The UI/UX succeeds if users:

  • understand services quickly

  • trust the company

  • feel impressed but not overwhelmed

  • navigate naturally

  • contact the company confidently

25. Final UX Vision

The final experience should feel like:

“A modern digital operating system built by a premium software engineering company.”

It should combine:

  • enterprise trust

  • futuristic aesthetics

  • cinematic interaction

  • technical confidence

  • usability-first design

1. App Flow Overview

Purpose

Define how users navigate, interact, and move through the ZABNIX digital experience.

  • create cinematic engagement

User Categories

Business Clients

Looking for:

  • software solutions

CTA Strategy

  • after trust-building sections

  • after service explanations

  • after projects

  • inside blogs

  • near footer

Main CTA Language

Start a Project

Let's Build Something Scalable

27. Error & Empty State Flow

Error States

Must remain:

  • minimal

  • clean

  • branded

Empty States

Examples:

  • no jobs available

  • no blog posts

  • no projects

Must still:

  • encourage interaction

  • maintain visual consistency

28. Performance Flow Considerations

Motion Reduction Logic

On lower-powered/mobile devices:

  • reduce particles

  • reduce blur

  • simplify animations

Maintain:

  • smoothness

  • responsiveness

  • readability

29. Accessibility Flow

Accessibility Requirements

Users must be able to:

  • navigate with keyboard

  • identify active sections

  • access forms easily

  • understand hierarchy clearly

30. Final Experience Flow Vision

The final application flow should feel like:

“Navigating a premium digital operating system built by a modern software engineering company.”

The experience must:

  • guide naturally

  • feel immersive

  • communicate authority

  • support business conversion

  • remain performant and usable

ZABNIX WEBSITE — BACKEND SCHEMA ARCHITECTURE

1. Backend Overview

Backend Type

Headless CMS Architecture

CMS Platform

Sanity

Backend Goals

The backend system must:

  • allow non-technical content management

  • support scalable structured content

  • support future extensibility

  • maintain clean content relationships

  • support SEO and dynamic rendering

  • integrate efficiently with Next.js frontend

2. Backend Architecture

System Flow

Sanity Studio (Admin)
        ↓
Structured Content
        ↓
Sanity Content Lake
        ↓
GROQ API Queries
        ↓
Next.js Frontend
        ↓
Vercel Deployment

3. Content Modeling Strategy

Content Principles

The schema architecture must:

  • remain modular

  • avoid duplication

  • support reusable references

  • separate presentation from content

  • support future scaling

Media Handling

Use Sanity Asset Pipeline.

Supported Media

Images
Videos
SVG
PDF
Documents

28. Relationship Architecture

Reference Strategy

Services ↔ Projects

Many-to-many relationship.

Industries ↔ Services

Many-to-many relationship.

Projects ↔ Case Studies

One-to-one relationship.

Blog ↔ Categories

Many-to-many relationship.

29. Slug Strategy

Slug Rules

All dynamic content must include:

slug.current

Used for:

  • SEO URLs

  • routing

  • metadata generation

30. Validation Rules

Required Validation

Examples

title required
slug unique
image required
SEO title max length
meta description length validation

31. Draft & Publishing Flow

Workflow

Draft
↓
Review
↓
Publish
↓
ISR Revalidation
↓
Live Website

32. Access Control

Roles

Admin
Editor
Content Manager
Recruitment Manager

Permissions

Admin

Full access.

Editor

Manage content only.

Recruitment Manager

Manage careers/applications.

33. Search & Filtering Support

Blog

  • categories

  • tags

  • search

Projects

  • technologies

  • industries

  • featured

Careers

  • department

  • employment type

  • location

34. Future Scalability Support

Backend Must Support Future

authentication
client dashboards
multi-language
multi-brand systems
tenant architecture
analytics integration
API expansion

35. API Query Strategy

Query Language

GROQ

Query Goals

  • minimal payload

  • optimized fetching

  • reusable fragments

  • structured references

Sanity uses GROQ for structured content querying in headless CMS workflows.

36. Revalidation Strategy

ISR Triggering

When content updates:

Sanity Webhook
↓
Vercel Revalidation
↓
Updated Page

37. Performance Rules

Backend Performance Goals

Avoid:

  • deeply nested references

  • oversized rich content

  • duplicate assets

Prefer:

  • modular references

  • reusable objects

  • optimized media

38. File Upload Strategy

Resume Uploads

For Careers:

  • PDF/DOC upload

  • size validation

  • secure storage

39. Analytics Support

Future Integrations

Google Analytics
Vercel Analytics
Hotjar
PostHog

40. Final Backend Vision

The backend system should function as:

“A scalable, structured, enterprise-grade content operating system for the ZABNIX digital ecosystem.”

It must:

  • empower non-technical editors

  • remain highly scalable

  • support cinematic frontend experiences

  • maintain performance and structure

  • prepare ZABNIX for future platform expansion


You said:

Implementation plan

ChatGPT said:

ZABNIX WEBSITE — IMPLEMENTATION PLAN

1. Implementation Overview

Objective

Build a production-ready, cinematic, CMS-driven company website for ZABNIX using a scalable modern architecture.

The implementation strategy focuses on:

  • scalability

  • maintainability

  • performance

  • visual quality

  • structured development workflow

2. Development Methodology

Recommended Workflow

Planning
↓
Architecture Setup
↓
CMS Setup
↓
Core UI System
↓
Page Development
↓
Animation Integration
↓
Optimization
↓
Deployment
↓
Testing & Refinement

3. Development Phases

PHASE 1 — PROJECT FOUNDATION

Goal

Set up the core project architecture.

Tasks

1. Initialize Next.js Project

Bash
npx create-next-app@latest zabnix

Options:

  • TypeScript → YES

  • Tailwind → YES

  • App Router → YES

  • ESLint → YES

2. Install Core Dependencies

UI + Motion

Bash
npm install framer-motion gsap lenis

CMS

Bash
npm install next-sanity sanity

Utilities

Bash
npm install clsx tailwind-merge lucide-react

3. Configure Project Structure

Create:

app/
components/
lib/
hooks/
types/
styles/
sanity/
public/

4. Configure Global Theme

Tasks:

  • dark/light system

  • color tokens

  • typography system

  • CSS variables

Deliverables

✅ Next.js initialized
✅ Folder architecture ready
✅ Tailwind configured
✅ Theme system configured

PHASE 2 — SANITY CMS SETUP

Goal

Build dynamic content infrastructure.

Tasks

1. Initialize Sanity

Bash
npm create sanity@latest

2. Configure Studio

Tasks:

  • project setup

  • dataset config

  • production environment

3. Create Schemas

Schemas:

settings
homepage
services
projects
products
team
leadership
careers
blog
seo

4. Configure Desk Structure

Custom CMS navigation:

  • grouped content

  • clean editor workflow

  • role-based organization

5. Connect Frontend to CMS

Tasks:

  • sanity client

  • GROQ queries

  • image builder

  • environment variables

Deliverables

✅ Sanity Studio running
✅ Schemas created
✅ Frontend connected
✅ Dynamic fetching operational

PHASE 3 — CORE UI SYSTEM

Goal

Build reusable design system.

Tasks

1. Layout Components

Build:

Navbar
Footer
Container
Section
PageWrapper

2. Core UI Components

Build:

Button
Card
Input
Badge
Modal
GlowWrapper

3. Typography System

Tasks:

  • responsive typography

  • utility classes

  • heading hierarchy

4. Responsive Layout System

Tasks:

  • desktop layouts

  • tablet adaptation

  • mobile optimization

Deliverables

✅ Reusable UI system
✅ Responsive layout foundation
✅ Typography system complete

PHASE 4 — HOMEPAGE DEVELOPMENT

Goal

Build the cinematic landing experience.

Sections

1. Loader

Features:

  • logo animation

  • electric blue pulse

  • cinematic transition

2. Hero Section

Features:

  • layered motion

  • animated grid

  • glow system

  • CTA buttons

  • cinematic typography

3. Ecosystem Section

Features:

  • interactive visualization

  • system architecture motion

4. Services Section

Features:

  • animated service cards

  • hover interactions

5. Industries Section

Features:

  • sector showcase

  • dynamic transitions

6. Products Section

Features:

  • WIP/internal products

  • interactive cards

7. Projects Section

Features:

  • cinematic previews

  • hover reveals

  • project highlights

8. Team Preview

Features:

  • leadership emphasis

  • animated cards

9. Contact CTA

Features:

  • conversion-focused layout

  • WhatsApp

  • Calendly

  • email

Deliverables

✅ Fully functional homepage
✅ Cinematic experience operational
✅ Mobile responsive

PHASE 5 — INTERNAL PAGE DEVELOPMENT

Goal

Build all secondary pages.

Pages

About

  • company story

  • ecosystem

  • leadership

Services

  • detailed service pages

  • dynamic content

Projects

  • portfolio system

  • project filtering

Products

  • product showcase

  • WIP products

Team

  • leadership

  • departments

  • profiles

Careers

  • job listing

  • application flow

Blog

  • article listing

  • categories

  • SEO pages

Contact

  • forms

  • integrations

  • map/contact methods

Deliverables

✅ All pages completed
✅ CMS integration functional

PHASE 6 — MOTION & INTERACTION SYSTEM

Goal

Implement cinematic UX.

Tasks

1. Lenis Integration

Features:

  • smooth scroll

  • controlled interpolation

  • mobile adaptation

2. Framer Motion System

Features:

  • reveal animations

  • stagger transitions

  • route transitions

3. GSAP Hero Sequences

Used ONLY for:

  • hero motion

  • scroll timelines

  • cinematic transitions

4. Page Transition System

Features:

  • smooth navigation

  • directional transitions

  • subtle cinematic wipes

Deliverables

✅ Smooth cinematic interactions
✅ Motion optimized
✅ Mobile-safe animations

Goal

Populate structured content.

Tasks

Add:

  • services

  • team members

  • leadership

  • projects

  • products

  • blogs

  • careers

Optimize:

  • images

  • metadata

  • slugs

  • SEO

Goal

Tasks

  • metadata

  • OpenGraph

  • sitemap

  • robots.txt

  • structured schema

Configure:

  • canonical URLs

  • dynamic SEO fields

  • blog metadata

Deliverables

Goal

Maintain cinematic UX without sacrificing performance.

Tasks

Optimize:

  • images

  • fonts

  • animations

  • bundle size

  • lazy loading

Lighthouse Targets

MetricGoal
Performance90+
Accessibility90+
SEO95+

Goal

Tasks

1. GitHub Repository

main branch
development branch

Platform:
Vercel

NEXT_PUBLIC_SANITY_PROJECT_ID
NEXT_PUBLIC_SANITY_DATASET
SANITY_API_TOKEN
NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL

Goal

Tasks

Analytics

Monitoring

4. Suggested Timeline

PhaseEstimated Time
Foundation1–2 days
CMS Setup1–2 days
Core UI System2–4 days
Homepage3–5 days
Internal Pages4–7 days
Motion System2–4 days
SEO + Optimization1–2 days
Testing + Deployment1–2 days

5. Recommended Development Order

STRICT ORDER

1. Foundation
2. CMS
3. UI System
4. Homepage
5. Internal Pages
6. Motion
7. SEO
8. Optimization
9. Testing
10. Deployment

6. Critical Engineering Rules

DO NOT

❌ overuse GSAP
❌ build massive Three.js scenes
❌ animate everything
❌ ignore mobile performance
❌ mix business logic into UI

ALWAYS

✅ isolate motion logic
✅ optimize assets
✅ keep schemas modular
✅ maintain component reusability
✅ prioritize readability

7. Final Implementation Vision

The final implementation should result in:

“A scalable, cinematic, enterprise-grade digital platform that positions ZABNIX as a premium modern software engineering company.”

The implementation must balance:

  • futuristic aesthetics

  • technical credibility

  • smooth UX

  • scalability

  • maintainability

  • performance


ChatGPT can make mistakes. Check important info.
